1: hallĕlû ʾet-yhwh kol-gôyim šabbĕḥûhû kol-hāʾummîm — "praise the LORD, all nations! Extol/laud him, all peoples!" — Ps 117 is the shortest psalm and the shortest chapter in the Bible. Its two commands address not Israel but the nations: gôyim (nations, Gentiles) and ʾummîm (peoples, tribes, ethnic groups) — the entire sweep of humanity. The verb šābaḥ (to praise, extol, laud) appears relatively rarely in the Psalter and has the connotation of public acclamation. Paul quotes this verse in Rom 15:11 as his fourth OT proof-text that God always intended the Gentiles to praise him — alongside Deut 32:43, 2 Sam 22:50, and Isa 11:10. The universal call is not a post-Resurrection innovation; it is embedded in the Psalter's own vision.
2: kî gābar ʿālênû ḥasdô wĕʾĕmet-yhwh lĕʿôlām hallĕlûyāh — "for his steadfast love (ḥesed) has prevailed/been mighty over us, and the faithfulness (ʾĕmet) of the LORD is forever. Praise the LORD!" — gābar (to be strong, prevail, be mighty, overcome) is an unusual verb to pair with ḥesed. Normally ḥesed is described as "great" or "enduring"; here it "prevails" — it overcomes, triumphs, wins. The two pillars of divine character — ḥesed (covenant love) and ʾĕmet (faithfulness, reliability) — recur from Exod 34:6 throughout the Psalter. Here they are the grounds on which the nations are called to praise: not Israel's military victories but YHWH's relentless covenant love, which is mighty enough to claim even the Gentiles.
